global.title: Swarm Data Model

coreIdeas.contextPhrase: Foundational: Swarm exposes two primitives — /bytes (immutable) and feeds (mutable). Every data-model note builds from here.
SOCs give each Ethereum account a writable namespace — the key insight that makes feeds possible.
Feeds = sequences of SOCs at predictable addresses. The concrete definition the rest of the model uses.
4KB chunks + Merkle hash tree are the physical storage layer under /bytes.
Mantaray manifests let Swarm resolve paths under a single reference — the shape Connect's user/drive/doc manifests use.
The three-tier manifest hierarchy (user → drive → doc) — Connect's end-to-end data model map.
User manifest feed details — top of the hierarchy.
Drive manifest feed details — documents + folder tree.
Document manifest feed details — latestRevision per scope + ordered operation batches.
Topic namespace convention ph:v2:<category>:<scope> — how keys are chosen consistently.
Per-document feed lookup: ph:v2:doc:<id> -> manifest -> operation batches.
Versioned ph:v2 topic namespace spans doc, drive, and collab feeds.
Collaboration index feed — participants + op-feed topics, powering live collab.
User/drive manifest reference — index of drives and child documents.
Why only 64-char hashes land in feeds: encrypted manifests live in /bytes.
Encryption at the /bytes layer keeps feeds plain — a key correctness invariant.
Contradicts earlier epoch-feed assumptions — important for ruling out dead paths.
Per-topic write locks are the operational reality of monotonic SOC indices.
Feed throughput is neighborhood-bound — capacity-planning guardrail.

global.description: How Connect encodes its state on Swarm: feeds, manifests, SOCs, content-addressed chunks, and the topic namespace that ties them together.
global.orientation: This MOC groups the primitives and shapes that form Connect's on-Swarm data model. At the lowest layer Swarm exposes two primitives — immutable /bytes and mutable owner-signed feeds built on Single Owner Chunks. Above that Connect layers a three-tier manifest hierarchy (user → drive → document), a versioned ph:v2 topic namespace, and specialized feeds (public profile, collab index, chat history). Notes here also cover operational realities — per-topic write locks, feed-backing stamp choices, the death of epoch feeds on bee-js 11, and why manifests are uploaded encrypted to /bytes with only their hashes inlined into feeds.
